articles.headline: PM Inaugurates Phase-II of Bharatmala Pariyojana, Boosts Connectivity and Economic Growth
articles.explainer:
• Prime Minister launches the second phase of the ambitious Bharatmala Pariyojana, focusing on developing economic corridors, inter-corridor and feeder routes.
• The new phase aims to further reduce logistics costs, enhance freight movement efficiency, and integrate remote regions into the national economic grid.
• Project includes development of 5,000 km of expressways and 15,000 km of national highways, leveraging advanced construction technologies.
• Emphasizes multi-modal connectivity under the Gati Shakti National Master Plan, connecting ports, industrial hubs, and agricultural production centers.
• Expected to create millions of jobs and catalyze socio-economic development across states, particularly in backward areas.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure (Roads, Ports, Railways, etc.)
Investment Models
Growth & Development
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: Bharatmala Pariyojana: Umbrella program for highway sector, focusing on optimizing efficiency of freight and passenger movement.
Phase-I (2017): Targeted 34,800 km of national highways.
Phase-II: Focuses on new economic corridors, inter-corridor, and feeder routes, aiming for enhanced multi-modal connectivity.
Gati Shakti National Master Plan: Integrated planning for infrastructure projects, reducing delays and improving coordination.
NIP: Outlay of ₹111 lakh crore for infrastructure projects by 2025.
mainsPoints: Role of infrastructure in economic growth: Facilitates trade, reduces transport costs, boosts manufacturing and agriculture.
Addressing regional disparities: Connecting remote areas to mainstream economy, promoting inclusive growth.
Enhancing logistics efficiency: Reduction in logistics costs is crucial for India's competitiveness in global trade.
Challenges in infrastructure development: Land acquisition, environmental clearances, funding models, project delays.
Impact on employment generation and socio-economic indicators.
articles.imageDescription: Prime Minister inaugurating a section of a newly constructed expressway under Bharatmala Pariyojana, with dignitaries and public present.
articles.trivia: Bharatmala Pariyojana is the second largest highway project in India after the National Highway Development Project (NHDP).

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Greater Transparency in Electoral Bonds Scheme
articles.explainer:
• A parliamentary standing committee submitted its report recommending reforms to the Electoral Bonds scheme to enhance transparency in political funding.
• The panel urged the Election Commission of India (ECI) and the government to review the anonymity clause, suggesting disclosure of donor identities to a regulatory body.
• Recommendations include setting a cap on corporate donations and ensuring timely disclosure of contributions received by political parties.
• The report highlights concerns regarding potential misuse of funds and the need for a level playing field in elections.
• Emphasizes strengthening the Representation of People's Act (RPA) to bring more accountability in political finance.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Transparency and accountability
Electoral reforms
Pressure groups and formal/informal associations
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds: Introduced in 2017, operationalized in 2018, allows anonymous donations to political parties.
Denominations: Available in multiples of ₹1,000 to ₹1 crore from SBI.
Eligibility: Only political parties registered under Section 29A of RPA, 1951, and secured at least 1% votes in last general/state election.
Key issues: Anonymity of donors, potential for quid pro quo, impact on free and fair elections.
Supreme Court: Petitions challenging the scheme's validity are ongoing.
mainsPoints: Need for electoral reforms: To ensure free, fair, and transparent elections, crucial for democratic health.
Transparency in political funding: Debate over anonymity vs. donor disclosure, balancing privacy with public interest.
Impact on democracy: How opaque funding can influence policy-making and create an uneven playing field.
Role of ECI and judiciary: Their efforts to bring reforms and ensure accountability.
International best practices: Comparing India's system with other democracies.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a parliamentary report, symbolizing legal and legislative scrutiny.
articles.trivia: The first electoral bonds were issued in March 2018. The highest value bond is ₹1 crore.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Prioritizes Growth Amid Easing Inflation Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive time.
• The decision reflects a cautious approach, balancing the need to control inflation with supporting economic growth in the face of global uncertainties.
• RBI noted a moderation in core inflation, while food inflation remains a key monitorable, influencing overall price stability.
• The central bank reaffirmed its 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance, indicating a readiness to act if inflation pressures resurface.
• Analysts suggest this pause provides stability for businesses and consumers, encouraging investment and consumption.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: Repo Rate: Rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
MPC: Six-member body (3 RBI, 3 GoI) responsible for fixing benchmark interest rate.
Inflation Target: RBI's mandate is to maintain inflation within 4% +/- 2% band.
Stance: 'Withdrawal of accommodation' means reducing liquidity from the system.
Other tools: CRR, SLR, Open Market Operations (OMOs).
mainsPoints: Role of RBI in economic management: Maintaining price stability, supporting growth, managing financial system.
Challenges of inflation vs. growth: The dilemma faced by central banks in balancing these two objectives.
Impact of global factors: Geopolitical tensions, commodity prices, and global economic slowdown on India's monetary policy.
Transmission of monetary policy: How changes in repo rate affect lending rates and overall economic activity.
Significance of forward guidance by central banks.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India headquarters building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016 and is headed by the RBI Governor.

articles.headline: India, Japan Deepen Strategic Partnership with Focus on Indo-Pacific Security and Green Economy
articles.explainer:
• India and Japan held their annual strategic dialogue, reaffirming their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, including joint exercises and technology transfer, to counter emerging regional security challenges.
• Both nations pledged to bolster economic ties, particularly in green technologies, renewable energy, and resilient supply chains.
• Japan reiterated its support for India's infrastructure development and high-speed rail projects.
• The partnership is seen as a cornerstone for regional stability and economic prosperity, aligning with India's Act East Policy.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-Japan relations: Upgraded to 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership' in 2014.
Quad: Informal strategic dialogue between India, USA, Japan, and Australia.
Defense cooperation: Malabar naval exercises, Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement (ACSA).
Economic cooperation: Japan is a major investor in India, particularly in infrastructure (e.g., Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail).
FOIP: Japan's vision for the Indo-Pacific, aligning with India's SAGAR doctrine.
mainsPoints: Importance of Indo-Pacific for India: Geopolitical significance, trade routes, countering China's influence.
Role of India-Japan partnership: As pillars of stability in the region, promoting rule-based order.
Economic dimension: Collaboration in emerging technologies, sustainable development, and resilient supply chains.
Challenges: Maritime security, territorial disputes, climate change, and economic vulnerabilities.
India's Act East Policy: Japan as a key partner in integrating India with East and Southeast Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed during a bilateral meeting.
articles.trivia: Japan is the only country with which India holds a 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue involving foreign and defense ministers.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-07
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the Earth Observation Satellite (EOS-07) onboard the PSLV-C58 rocket from Sriharikota.
• EOS-07 is an advanced remote sensing satellite designed to provide high-resolution imagery for various applications, including urban planning, agriculture, and disaster management.
• The mission also carried several co-passenger satellites, including student-built payloads, showcasing India's growing space capabilities.
• This launch further strengthens India's indigenous remote sensing capabilities and its commitment to utilizing space technology for national development.
• The satellite will contribute significantly to climate monitoring and environmental assessment efforts.
gsSubTopics: Awareness in the fields of Space
Indigenous technology and developing new technology
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
prelimsPoints: EOS series: A new nomenclature for ISRO's Earth Observation Satellites.
PSLV: Workhorse launch vehicle of ISRO, known for its reliability and versatility.
Applications of remote sensing: Agriculture (crop yield, soil health), urban planning, forestry, water resource management, disaster management.
Sriharikota: Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, India's primary spaceport.
Co-passenger payloads: Demonstrates ISRO's capability to launch multiple satellites in a single mission.
mainsPoints: Contribution of ISRO to national development: Space applications in communication, navigation, meteorology, and resource management.
India's self-reliance in space technology: From launch vehicles to satellite manufacturing and applications.
Commercialization of space: Role of NewSpace India Limited (NSIL) in promoting private sector participation.
Space diplomacy: India's role in international space cooperation and capacity building for other nations.
Challenges and future prospects: Developing next-gen launch vehicles, human spaceflight (Gaganyaan), space debris management.
articles.imageDescription: A PSLV rocket lifting off from the launchpad, with smoke trails.
articles.trivia: ISRO is one of the six government space agencies in the world that possess full launch capabilities.

articles.headline: National Green Tribunal Directs States to Expedite Waste Management Infrastructure
articles.explainer:
• The National Green Tribunal (NGT) issued strict directives to state governments and Union Territories to accelerate the establishment of proper waste management infrastructure.
• The order emphasizes the need for scientific processing of solid and liquid waste, in compliance with the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016, and other environmental norms.
• NGT highlighted the severe environmental and public health risks posed by untreated waste and illegal dumping sites.
• States are mandated to submit action plans and progress reports, with penalties for non-compliance.
• The ruling aims to ensure sustainable waste management practices and achieve Swachh Bharat Mission goals.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation
Environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: NGT: Established in 2010 under NGT Act, 2010, for effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection.
SWM Rules, 2016: Replaced Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000; emphasize segregation, collection, processing, and disposal.
Polluter Pays Principle: NGT often applies this principle.
Swachh Bharat Mission (Urban & Rural): Government's flagship program for cleanliness and sanitation.
Types of waste: Biodegradable, non-biodegradable, hazardous, e-waste, plastic waste.
mainsPoints: Challenges of waste management in India: Rapid urbanization, lack of infrastructure, informal sector, public apathy, financial constraints.
Environmental and health impacts: Landfill fires, groundwater contamination, air pollution, disease spread.
Role of judiciary (NGT): Active role in environmental governance, ensuring compliance with laws.
Sustainable waste management solutions: Segregation at source, recycling, composting, waste-to-energy plants, circular economy principles.
Government initiatives: Swachh Bharat Mission, Plastic Waste Management Rules, EPR.
articles.imageDescription: A visual representation of waste segregation bins for different types of waste.
articles.trivia: India generates approximately 1.5 lakh metric tonnes of solid waste every day.

articles.headline: India Boosts Border Infrastructure Amidst Persistent Cross-Border Challenges
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence announced accelerated development of critical border infrastructure, including roads, bridges, and advanced surveillance systems.
• This move comes in response to ongoing challenges along India's northern and eastern borders, aiming to enhance troop mobility and logistical support.
• Emphasis is placed on integrating cutting-edge technology like drones, AI-powered surveillance, and satellite communication for real-time monitoring.
• The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) is a key agency spearheading these projects in difficult terrains.
• The initiative is crucial for national security and for strengthening India's strategic posture in contested regions.
gsSubTopics: Border areas and their management
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Security challenges and their management in border areas
prelimsPoints: BRO: Established in 1960, develops and maintains road networks in border areas and friendly neighboring countries.
Key borders: India shares land borders with 7 countries (Pakistan, Afghanistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Myanmar).
Challenges: Infiltration, smuggling, terrorism, territorial disputes, adverse weather conditions.
Surveillance technology: Drones, CCTV, night vision devices, ground sensors, satellite imagery.
Border guarding forces: BSF (Pakistan, Bangladesh), ITBP (China), SSB (Nepal, Bhutan), Assam Rifles (Myanmar).
mainsPoints: Importance of border management: Crucial for national security, preventing illegal activities, and maintaining sovereignty.
Infrastructure development in border areas: Facilitates troop movement, aids local population, and promotes economic integration.
Technological advancements: Role of AI, drones, and satellite tech in enhancing border surveillance and response.
Challenges in border management: Porous borders, diverse terrain, cross-border terrorism, human trafficking.
Comprehensive approach: Involving security forces, local administration, and public participation for effective border management.
articles.imageDescription: A newly constructed road winding through a mountainous border region.
articles.trivia: The BRO constructs and maintains roads in 19 states and 3 Union Territories, including 8 out of 10 border states.

articles.headline: Government Launches 'Skill India Digital 2.0' to Bridge Skill Gap and Boost Employment
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship launched 'Skill India Digital 2.0', an upgraded digital platform for skilling, reskilling, and upskilling the workforce.
• The platform integrates various government skill development initiatives and offers industry-relevant courses, certifications, and job matching services.
• It aims to provide accessible and affordable vocational training to millions of youth, especially in rural and underserved areas.
• Focus areas include emerging technologies like AI, data science, cybersecurity, and green skills, aligning with future industry demands.
• The initiative seeks to leverage India's demographic dividend by enhancing employability and promoting entrepreneurship.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Skill India Mission: Launched in 2015 to train over 40 crore people in India in different skills by 2022 (ongoing).
NSDC: Public-private partnership, facilitates skill development.
PMKVY: Flagship scheme of MSDE, provides short-term training and recognition of prior learning.
Digital platforms: Crucial for scaling skill development and reaching remote areas.
Green skills: Skills required for sustainable development and green economy.
mainsPoints: Importance of skill development: Crucial for economic growth, reducing unemployment, and leveraging India's demographic dividend.
Challenges in skill ecosystem: Mismatch between skills and industry demand, quality of training, formal vs. informal sector.
Role of digital platforms: Enhancing accessibility, affordability, and scalability of skill training.
Future of work: Preparing workforce for automation, AI, and emerging technologies.
Promoting entrepreneurship: Skilling for self-employment and job creation.
articles.imageDescription: A group of young Indian professionals engaging in digital learning on laptops.
articles.trivia: India has one of the world's largest working-age populations, making skill development critical for its economic future.

articles.headline: Remembering Raja Ravi Varma: A Pioneer of Modern Indian Art on His Birth Anniversary
articles.explainer:
• Today marks the birth anniversary of Raja Ravi Varma (1848-1906), a celebrated Indian painter and artist.
• Varma is renowned for his fusion of traditional Indian aesthetics with European academic art techniques, particularly oil painting.
• His works predominantly depicted Hindu deities and scenes from Indian epics and mythology, making classical art accessible to the masses through oleographs.
• He played a pivotal role in shaping the visual culture of modern India and influenced subsequent generations of artists.
• His art captured the imagination of a nation, contributing significantly to the cultural nationalism of the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Born: Kilimanoor, Travancore (present-day Kerala) on January 8, 1848.
Style: Blended European realistic techniques (perspective, chiaroscuro) with Indian subject matter.
Famous works: 'Lady in the Moonlight', 'Shakuntala', 'Damayanti and the Swan', 'Goddess Lakshmi', 'Saraswati'.
Contribution: Pioneered oleography (chromo-lithography) in India, making art affordable and widespread.
Awards: Conferred 'Kaisar-i-Hind' gold medal by Viceroy Lord Curzon in 1904.
mainsPoints: Evolution of modern Indian art: Raja Ravi Varma's role as a bridge between traditional and modern art forms.
Impact on cultural nationalism: His popularization of mythological themes contributed to a sense of national identity.
Influence of Western art: How Indian artists adapted European techniques to local contexts.
Role of patronage: Support from princely states in fostering art and culture.
Challenges faced by artists in colonial India.
articles.imageDescription: A famous painting by Raja Ravi Varma depicting a mythological scene.
articles.trivia: Raja Ravi Varma established his own lithographic press in Mumbai in 1894 to print oleographs of his paintings.

articles.headline: IMD Predicts Above-Normal Winter Rainfall in Northern India, Concerns for Agriculture
articles.explainer:
• The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecasted above-normal winter rainfall for January and February across several parts of Northern India.
• This prediction is primarily attributed to a series of active Western Disturbances expected to affect the region.
• While beneficial for some Rabi crops, excessive or unseasonal rainfall raises concerns about potential crop damage, especially for mature crops.
• Farmers are advised to take precautionary measures, and agricultural experts are monitoring the situation closely.
• The changing rainfall patterns highlight the broader impacts of climate change on India's agricultural sector.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
prelimsPoints: Western Disturbances: Extra-tropical storms originating in the Mediterranean region, bringing sudden winter rain to NW India.
Rabi crops: Sown in winter (Oct-Dec) and harvested in spring (Apr-May), e.g., wheat, barley, mustard, gram.
Kharif crops: Sown in monsoon (June-July) and harvested in autumn (Sept-Oct), e.g., rice, maize, jowar, bajra.
IMD: Principal agency responsible for meteorological observations, weather forecasting, and seismology in India.
Impact of unseasonal rain: Crop lodging, pest attacks, delayed harvesting, reduced yield.
mainsPoints: Impact of climate change on agriculture: Changing rainfall patterns, extreme weather events, effects on crop cycles and yields.
Agricultural resilience: Need for crop diversification, climate-resilient varieties, and improved irrigation techniques.
Role of weather forecasting: Importance of accurate and timely advisories for farmers and disaster management.
Government schemes: PM Fasal Bima Yojana, National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) to support farmers.
Food security implications: Ensuring stable agricultural production amidst climatic variability.
articles.imageDescription: A wheat field in Northern India with light rain falling.
articles.trivia: The term 'Western Disturbance' was coined by Indian meteorologists in the early 20th century.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Validity of EWS Quota in State-Funded Institutions, Reaffirms Constitutional Basis
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, upheld the constitutional validity of the 103rd Amendment Act, which introduced a 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS).
• The majority ruling emphasized that the quota does not violate the basic structure of the Constitution and serves the objective of social justice for the economically disadvantaged.
• The court clarified that the EWS quota is a separate category and does not infringe upon the existing reservations for SCs, STs, and OBCs.
• This verdict solidifies the legal framework for affirmative action based on economic criteria, alongside social and educational backwardness.
• The decision has significant implications for admissions to state-funded educational institutions and public employment.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: 103rd Amendment Act, 2019: Inserted Articles 15(6) and 16(6) to provide 10% EWS reservation.
EWS criteria: Gross annual family income below ₹8 lakh, exclusion criteria for land/property ownership.
Indra Sawhney case (1992): Imposed a 50% ceiling on total reservations (excluding special circumstances).
Basic Structure Doctrine: Propounded in Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), limits Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.
Challenges to EWS: Argued it violates basic structure by exceeding 50% cap and excluding SC/ST/OBC from EWS.
mainsPoints: Debates around reservation policy: Balancing affirmative action, merit, and equality of opportunity.
Concept of social justice: Expanding its scope to include economic backwardness alongside social and educational.
Judicial review and constitutional amendments: Role of Supreme Court in interpreting and upholding the Constitution.
Implications for public policy: Impact on education, employment, and social stratification.
Arguments for and against EWS quota: Addressing poverty, potential for further fragmentation of society.
articles.imageDescription: The Supreme Court of India building.
articles.trivia: The 103rd Amendment was the first reservation based solely on economic criteria, distinct from social and educational backwardness.

articles.headline: Pre-Budget Consultations Highlight Need for Green Energy Transition Investments
articles.explainer:
• In the run-up to the Union Budget, pre-budget consultations with industry leaders and experts emphasized significant investments in green energy transition.
• Stakeholders called for policy support, fiscal incentives, and R&D funding to accelerate the adoption of renewable energy, green hydrogen, and electric vehicles.
• Discussions focused on India's climate commitments and the potential for green industries to drive economic growth and job creation.
• The Ministry of Finance acknowledged the strategic importance of energy transition for energy security and environmental sustainability.
• Recommendations included establishing a dedicated green finance fund and streamlining regulatory approvals for clean energy projects.
gsSubTopics: Investment models
Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
prelimsPoints: India's climate targets: Net-zero by 2070, 50% installed capacity from non-fossil fuels by 2030.
Green Hydrogen Mission: Aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
IREDA: Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency, a PSU under MNRE.
PLI scheme: Production Linked Incentive scheme for various sectors including advanced chemistry cell battery manufacturing.
Carbon pricing: A policy tool to internalize the cost of carbon emissions.
mainsPoints: India's climate commitments: Balancing economic growth with environmental sustainability and international obligations.
Economic opportunities in green transition: Job creation, new industries, export potential.
Challenges in energy transition: Funding gap, technological hurdles, grid integration, land acquisition.
Role of government policy: Fiscal incentives, regulatory framework, R&D support.
Energy security: Reducing reliance on fossil fuel imports through domestic renewable energy production.
articles.imageDescription: Wind turbines and solar panels against a backdrop of a clear sky, symbolizing green energy.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest energy consuming country in the world.

articles.headline: India Engages with BIMSTEC Nations on Regional Connectivity Projects
articles.explainer:
• India participated in a high-level meeting with BIMSTEC (Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation) member states to discuss accelerating regional connectivity projects.
• Focus areas include improving road, rail, and maritime links, as well as enhancing digital and energy connectivity across the Bay of Bengal region.
• These initiatives are crucial for boosting intra-regional trade, promoting tourism, and fostering people-to-people exchanges.
• India reiterated its commitment to BIMSTEC as a key pillar of its 'Neighbourhood First' and 'Act East' policies.
• The discussions also touched upon facilitating easier movement of goods and services, aligning with regional integration goals.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
India and its neighbourhood- relations
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: BIMSTEC: Sub-regional organization established in 1997, comprising 7 member states (Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Thailand).
Objective: Technical and economic cooperation among member countries bordering the Bay of Bengal.
Significance for India: Bridges South and Southeast Asia, vital for Act East Policy and regional security.
Connectivity projects: Kaladan Multi-Modal Transit Transport Project, Trilateral Highway.
Difference from SAARC: Excludes Pakistan, more focused on Bay of Bengal region.
mainsPoints: Importance of BIMSTEC for India's foreign policy: Enhancing regional influence, economic integration, and countering China's growing presence.
Challenges to regional cooperation: Geopolitical complexities, funding issues, infrastructure gaps, security concerns.
Potential benefits of enhanced connectivity: Boost to trade, tourism, people-to-people contact, disaster management cooperation.
Comparison with other regional groupings (SAARC, ASEAN): BIMSTEC's unique advantages and focus.
Role in maritime security and blue economy development.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of BIMSTEC member countries arranged in a meeting setting.
articles.trivia: BIMSTEC members collectively account for 22% of the global population and have a combined GDP of over US$3.8 trillion.

articles.headline: New National Health Policy Focuses on Preventive Healthcare and Digital Health Integration
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare unveiled a comprehensive new National Health Policy, emphasizing a shift towards preventive and promotive healthcare.
• The policy aims to strengthen primary healthcare services, making them the first point of contact for most health needs, reducing the burden on tertiary care.
• A significant focus is on integrating digital health technologies, including telemedicine, AI-driven diagnostics, and the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
• It also outlines strategies for enhancing health infrastructure, human resources, and ensuring equitable access to quality healthcare across all sections of society.
• The policy seeks to achieve Universal Health Coverage (UHC) and improve key health indicators by 2030.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
E-governance
prelimsPoints: National Health Policy (NHP) 2017: Previous policy, aimed at achieving UHC.
Ayushman Bharat: Two pillars - Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) and Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY).
ABDM: Aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
Preventive care: Focus on lifestyle diseases, nutrition, sanitation, immunization.
Health indicators: Infant Mortality Rate (IMR), Maternal Mortality Rate (MMR), Life Expectancy.
mainsPoints: Challenges in public health in India: Access, affordability, quality, rural-urban divide, shortage of healthcare professionals.
Importance of preventive healthcare: Reduces disease burden, lowers healthcare costs, improves quality of life.
Role of digital health: Enhancing accessibility, efficiency, and data-driven decision making in healthcare.
Achieving Universal Health Coverage: Strategies, funding models, and challenges.
Government's role in health policy: Regulatory framework, public health programs, international collaborations.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with a patient via a telemedicine platform on a screen.
articles.trivia: India's public health spending has historically been among the lowest globally, but recent policies aim to increase it.

articles.headline: Ministry of Environment Unveils New Policy for E-waste Management, Emphasizing Circular Economy
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) introduced a new comprehensive policy framework for E-waste Management.
• The policy aims to promote a circular economy model by encouraging reuse, repair, and recycling of electronic waste.
• It expands the scope of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) to cover a wider range of electronic products and sets stricter collection targets.
• Measures are included to formalize the informal e-waste recycling sector, ensuring safer working conditions and environmentally sound practices.
• The policy seeks to mitigate the adverse environmental and health impacts of e-waste and conserve valuable resources.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation
Environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
prelimsPoints: E-waste: Discarded electrical or electronic devices.
E-waste (Management) Rules, 2016 (and amendments): Govern e-waste handling in India.
EPR: Producers are responsible for the collection and channelization of e-waste.
Circular Economy: A model of production and consumption, which involves sharing, leasing, reusing, repairing, refurbishing and recycling existing materials and products as long as possible.
Basel Convention: International treaty on transboundary movements of hazardous wastes and their disposal.
mainsPoints: Challenges of e-waste in India: Growing volume, informal recycling sector, health hazards, resource depletion, lack of infrastructure.
Importance of circular economy: Reducing waste, conserving resources, creating green jobs, reducing environmental footprint.
Role of EPR: Shifting responsibility to producers for end-of-life management of products.
Policy measures: Collection targets, awareness campaigns, formalizing informal sector, technological solutions.
International cooperation and best practices in e-waste management.
articles.imageDescription: A pile of discarded electronic devices, representing e-waste.
articles.trivia: India is one of the top five e-waste generating countries in the world.

articles.headline: CERT-In Issues High-Severity Alert on New Ransomware Threat Targeting Critical Infrastructure
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) has issued a high-severity alert regarding a sophisticated new ransomware variant targeting critical infrastructure sectors.
• The ransomware, dubbed 'CipherLock,' exploits vulnerabilities in network systems and encrypts vital data, demanding payment for decryption keys.
• CERT-In advises organizations, especially in energy, finance, and healthcare, to immediately implement robust security patches, multi-factor authentication, and data backup protocols.
• The alert highlights the increasing frequency and sophistication of cyberattacks, posing significant risks to national security and economic stability.
• Government agencies are working with private sector partners to enhance cybersecurity resilience and share threat intelligence.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention
prelimsPoints: CERT-In: National agency for responding to computer security incidents.
Ransomware: Type of malware that encrypts a victim's files and demands a ransom payment.
Critical Infrastructure: Assets (physical and virtual) essential for the functioning of a society and economy.
IT Act, 2000: Primary law in India dealing with cybercrime and e-commerce.
Data Protection Bill: Under consideration to strengthen data privacy and security.
mainsPoints: Growing cyber threats to national security: Espionage, sabotage, data theft, financial fraud.
Vulnerability of critical infrastructure: Potential for widespread disruption and economic damage.
Need for robust cybersecurity framework: Policy, legal, technological, and human resource aspects.
International cooperation in cybersecurity: Sharing threat intelligence, combating cross-border cybercrime.
Role of individuals and organizations: Best practices for cyber hygiene and incident response.
articles.imageDescription: A digital padlock icon overlaid on a network diagram, symbolizing cybersecurity threats.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top countries targeted by ransomware attacks globally.

articles.headline: Implementation of National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 Gains Momentum in Higher Education
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education announced significant progress in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, particularly in the higher education sector.
• Key reforms include the establishment of multidisciplinary universities, flexible curriculum choices, and emphasis on vocational education integration.
• The University Grants Commission (UGC) and All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) are actively facilitating the transition to NEP-aligned frameworks.
• Efforts are underway to promote digital learning platforms and internationalization of higher education, attracting global talent and collaboration.
• The policy aims to transform India into a global knowledge superpower by fostering holistic, equitable, and quality education.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
keyTerms: All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE)
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020: Replaced the 1986 policy; aims for universalization of education from pre-school to higher education.
Key features (higher education): Multidisciplinary approach, flexible curriculum, multiple entry/exit options, Academic Bank of Credits.
Regulatory bodies: UGC, AICTE, National Council for Teacher Education (NCTE).
MERUs: Model multidisciplinary institutions of global standards.
Digital initiatives: SWAYAM, SWAYAM Prabha, National Digital Library.
mainsPoints: Challenges and opportunities in Indian higher education: Quality, access, equity, funding, research output.
Transformative potential of NEP 2020: Fostering critical thinking, creativity, and interdisciplinary learning.
Role of vocational education: Bridging skill gaps and enhancing employability.
Internationalization of education: Attracting foreign universities, promoting student mobility, global competitiveness.
Ensuring equitable access to quality education for all sections of society.
articles.imageDescription: Students studying in a modern university library, symbolizing higher education.
articles.trivia: NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

articles.headline: Government Reviews MSP Policy to Ensure Fair Returns for Farmers Amidst Agricultural Reforms
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare initiated a comprehensive review of the Minimum Support Price (MSP) policy to ensure fair and remunerative prices for farmers.
• The review is part of broader agricultural reforms aimed at increasing farmers' income and making the agricultural sector more sustainable and market-oriented.
• Discussions include strengthening procurement mechanisms, diversifying crop patterns, and integrating technology in agricultural marketing.
• Farmers' organizations have submitted recommendations advocating for legal guarantees for MSP and considering cost+50% formula.
• The government aims to balance farmer welfare with food security and fiscal sustainability.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers; Minimum Support Prices
keyTerms: Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP)
prelimsPoints: MSP: Price at which government purchases crops from farmers to protect them from price fluctuations.
CACP: Recommends MSP for 22 mandated crops and Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for sugarcane.
Procurement: Primarily done by FCI for wheat and paddy.
MSP calculation: 'A2+FL' (actual paid out cost + imputed value of family labour) or 'C2' (comprehensive cost including imputed rent/interest on owned land/capital).
Swaminathan Committee recommendation: MSP should be at least 50% more than the weighted average cost of production (C2).
mainsPoints: Challenges in agricultural marketing: Fragmentation, lack of infrastructure, price volatility, exploitation by middlemen.
Debate over MSP: Effectiveness, fiscal burden, distortion of cropping patterns, environmental impact.
Farmer income: Strategies to double farmer income, diversification, value addition, non-farm activities.
Food security: Balancing farmer welfare with consumer prices and adequate food grain stocks.
Role of agricultural reforms: Empowering farmers, improving market access, leveraging e-NAM and FPOs.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer inspecting his crops in a field, symbolizing agricultural efforts.
articles.trivia: MSP was first introduced in India in 1966-67 for wheat to incentivize farmers during the Green Revolution.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Uncovers Ancient Settlement in Gujarat, Hinting at Pre-Harappan Links
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of an ancient settlement site in Gujarat, potentially dating back to the pre-Harappan period.
• Initial excavations have revealed pottery shards, tools, and structural remains, suggesting a well-organized community with possible links to early Indus Valley Civilization phases.
• The find is significant for understanding the cultural evolution and migration patterns in Western India prior to the mature Harappan period.
• Researchers believe the site could offer crucial insights into the transition from early farming communities to complex urban centers.
• The discovery underscores Gujarat's rich archaeological heritage and its role in ancient Indian history.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
History of India and Indian National Movement
prelimsPoints: ASI: Founded in 1861, premier organization for archaeological research and heritage preservation in India.
Indus Valley Civilization (IVC): One of the world's earliest urban civilizations (c. 2500–1900 BCE).
Major Harappan sites in Gujarat: Lothal, Dholavira, Rangpur, Rojdi, Surkotada.
Pre-Harappan sites: Amri, Kot Diji (Pakistan), Kalibangan (Rajasthan).
Artefacts: Pottery, beads, seals, tools provide insights into daily life, trade, and culture.
mainsPoints: Importance of archaeological discoveries: Reconstructing ancient history, understanding cultural evolution, challenging existing narratives.
Gujarat's role in IVC: Coastal trade, unique settlement patterns, interaction with other cultures.
Pre-Harappan phase: Understanding the antecedents and developmental stages of the mature Harappan civilization.
Challenges in archaeological research: Preservation of sites, funding, modern development pressures.
Contribution to cultural tourism and national identity.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists carefully excavating an ancient site, revealing pottery fragments.
articles.trivia: Dholavira in Gujarat is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the largest Harappan cities.

articles.headline: Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) Crucial for Achieving India's Development Goals, Says NITI Aayog
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog emphasized the critical role of Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) in mobilizing resources and expertise to achieve India's ambitious development goals.
• A new framework is being developed to streamline the PPP process, enhance risk-sharing mechanisms, and attract greater private investment in infrastructure and social sectors.
• Focus areas include smart cities, renewable energy, affordable housing, and digital infrastructure, where private sector efficiency can complement public sector reach.
• The government aims to foster an environment conducive to long-term private sector participation, ensuring transparency and accountability.
• PPPs are seen as vital for accelerating project implementation and improving service delivery, particularly in achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
gsSubTopics: Investment models
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: PPP: Collaboration between government and private sector for projects/services.
NITI Aayog: Premier policy 'think tank' of the Government of India, providing directional and policy inputs.
Types of PPP models: BOT (Build-Operate-Transfer), BOO (Build-Own-Operate), HAM (Hybrid Annuity Model).
VGF: Financial support from government to make PPP projects commercially viable.
India Infrastructure Finance Company Limited (IIFCL): Provides long-term finance to infrastructure projects.
mainsPoints: Advantages of PPPs: Mobilizing private capital, efficiency in project execution, risk transfer, innovation, improved service delivery.
Challenges in PPPs: Project structuring, risk allocation, regulatory hurdles, land acquisition, political interference, transparency issues.
Role in achieving SDGs: PPPs can accelerate progress in areas like infrastructure, clean energy, health, and education.
Government's role: Creating an enabling environment, robust regulatory framework, fair dispute resolution mechanism.
Case studies of successful and unsuccessful PPPs in India and lessons learned.
articles.imageDescription: A handshake between two individuals, symbolizing partnership, with a city skyline in the background.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest PPP markets in the world, especially in the infrastructure sector.

highlights: PM inaugurates Phase-II of Bharatmala Pariyojana, aiming to boost national connectivity and reduce logistics costs.
Parliamentary panel recommends reforms in the Electoral Bonds scheme for greater transparency in political funding.
RBI's Monetary Policy Committee maintains the repo rate at 6.5%, prioritizing growth amidst easing inflation concerns.
India and Japan deepen strategic partnership, focusing on Indo-Pacific security and green economy initiatives.
ISRO successfully launches Earth Observation Satellite EOS-07, enhancing India's remote sensing capabilities.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Bharatmala Pariyojana, consider the following statements:
1. It is a centrally sponsored scheme focusing on developing economic corridors and improving connectivity.
2. It aims to reduce logistics costs to 8% of GDP by 2030.
3. The project includes the development of coastal and port connectivity roads.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: Bharatmala Pariyojana is a centrally funded and managed scheme, not centrally sponsored. Statement 2 is correct: One of the key objectives of Bharatmala Pariyojana, aligned with the National Logistics Policy, is to reduce logistics costs to 8% of GDP by 2030. Statement 3 is correct: The project indeed includes developing coastal and port connectivity roads, along with economic corridors, inter-corridor and feeder routes, and border roads.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding Electoral Bonds in India:
1. Electoral Bonds can be purchased by any Indian citizen or entity incorporated in India.
2. The bonds are available for purchase only during specific periods notified by the Election Commission of India.
3. Only political parties registered under the Representation of People’s Act, 1951, and securing at least 1% of votes in the last general or state election, are eligible to receive electoral bonds.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Electoral Bonds can be purchased by a person who is a citizen of India or incorporated or established in India. Statement 2 is incorrect: The bonds are available for purchase for a period of ten days in the months of January, April, July and October, and an additional period of thirty days in a general election year, as notified by the Central Government, not ECI. Statement 3 is correct: This is one of the eligibility criteria for political parties to receive electoral bonds.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) in India?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body (three from RBI, three appointed by the government) that is entrusted with the task of fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) to achieve the inflation target within the band of +/- 2% of the 4% target.

prelimsMocks.question: With respect to the Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) series launched by ISRO, which of the following is NOT a primary application?
options: (c) Agricultural monitoring and crop yield estimation
prelimsMocks.explanation: Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) primarily focus on remote sensing applications for land, ocean, and atmospheric studies, which include urban planning, agriculture, disaster management, and environmental assessment. Navigational guidance for maritime vessels is primarily handled by navigation satellites like those under India's NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation) system, not Earth Observation Satellites.

prelimsMocks.question: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, which provides for reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS), inserted which of the following Articles into the Indian Constitution?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019, inserted Articles 15(6) and 16(6) in the Constitution, enabling the State to make special provisions, including reservation, for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) of citizens for admission to educational institutions and in public employment.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the significance of infrastructure development, particularly projects like Bharatmala Pariyojana, in achieving India's economic growth and regional integration goals. What challenges persist in their implementation?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.; Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Bharatmala Pariyojana and its objectives. Elaborate on its significance for economic growth (logistics, manufacturing, trade) and regional integration (connecting backward areas, border regions). Discuss challenges such as land acquisition, environmental clearances, funding constraints, inter-agency coordination, and social impact. Conclude with measures for effective implementation.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the efficacy and ethical implications of the Electoral Bonds scheme in promoting transparency in political funding. Suggest reforms that could balance the need for funding with democratic accountability.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act; Transparency and accountability and institutional and other measures; Electoral reforms.
mainsMocks.approach: Define Electoral Bonds and their stated objectives. Critically examine their efficacy in promoting transparency, highlighting concerns about anonymity and potential for quid pro quo. Discuss ethical implications for democracy and level playing field. Suggest reforms like disclosure of donor identities, limits on corporate donations, strengthening regulatory oversight, and exploring alternative funding mechanisms.

mainsMocks.question: India's 'Act East Policy' has evolved beyond economic engagement to encompass strategic and security dimensions. Discuss how India's deepening partnership with Japan reflects this evolution, especially in the context of Indo-Pacific security.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; India and its neighbourhood- relations.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Act East Policy and its transition from 'Look East'. Explain the strategic rationale for the Indo-Pacific region. Detail how the India-Japan partnership has expanded from economic ties to defense cooperation, maritime security, and multilateral engagements (e.g., Quad). Discuss shared values and concerns (e.g., rule of law, freedom of navigation, countering China's assertiveness). Conclude on the overall significance of this partnership for regional stability.

quickBytes.text: The Bharatmala Pariyojana aims to develop approximately 83,677 km of highways by 2022.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) is a constitutional body responsible for administering elections in India.
The current inflation targeting framework for RBI aims to keep CPI inflation at 4% with a +/- 2% band.
Japan is the largest provider of Official Development Assistance (ODA) to India.
PSLV-C58 is the 60th flight of ISRO's Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle.
The National Green Tribunal (NGT) has five places of sitting: Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, Chennai, and New Delhi.
Raja Ravi Varma was known for popularizing oil painting and oleography in India.
Western Disturbances are crucial for Rabi crop cultivation in North India.
The EWS quota was introduced through the 103rd Amendment to the Indian Constitution.
BIMSTEC stands for Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation.

GS1.headline: Remembering Raja Ravi Varma: A Pioneer of Modern Indian Art on His Birth Anniversary
GS1.explainer:
• Today marks the birth anniversary of Raja Ravi Varma (1848-1906), a celebrated Indian painter and artist.
• Varma is renowned for his fusion of traditional Indian aesthetics with European academic art techniques, particularly oil painting.
• His works predominantly depicted Hindu deities and scenes from Indian epics and mythology, making classical art accessible to the masses through oleographs.
• He played a pivotal role in shaping the visual culture of modern India and influenced subsequent generations of artists.
• His art captured the imagination of a nation, contributing significantly to the cultural nationalism of the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Born: Kilimanoor, Travancore (present-day Kerala) on January 8, 1848.
Style: Blended European realistic techniques (perspective, chiaroscuro) with Indian subject matter.
Famous works: 'Lady in the Moonlight', 'Shakuntala', 'Damayanti and the Swan', 'Goddess Lakshmi', 'Saraswati'.
Contribution: Pioneered oleography (chromo-lithography) in India, making art affordable and widespread.
Awards: Conferred 'Kaisar-i-Hind' gold medal by Viceroy Lord Curzon in 1904.
mainsPoints: Evolution of modern Indian art: Raja Ravi Varma's role as a bridge between traditional and modern art forms.
Impact on cultural nationalism: His popularization of mythological themes contributed to a sense of national identity.
Influence of Western art: How Indian artists adapted European techniques to local contexts.
Role of patronage: Support from princely states in fostering art and culture.
Challenges faced by artists in colonial India.
GS1.imageDescription: A famous painting by Raja Ravi Varma depicting a mythological scene.
GS1.trivia: Raja Ravi Varma established his own lithographic press in Mumbai in 1894 to print oleographs of his paintings.

GS1.headline: IMD Predicts Above-Normal Winter Rainfall in Northern India, Concerns for Agriculture
GS1.explainer:
• The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecasted above-normal winter rainfall for January and February across several parts of Northern India.
• This prediction is primarily attributed to a series of active Western Disturbances expected to affect the region.
• While beneficial for some Rabi crops, excessive or unseasonal rainfall raises concerns about potential crop damage, especially for mature crops.
• Farmers are advised to take precautionary measures, and agricultural experts are monitoring the situation closely.
• The changing rainfall patterns highlight the broader impacts of climate change on India's agricultural sector.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
prelimsPoints: Western Disturbances: Extra-tropical storms originating in the Mediterranean region, bringing sudden winter rain to NW India.
Rabi crops: Sown in winter (Oct-Dec) and harvested in spring (Apr-May), e.g., wheat, barley, mustard, gram.
Kharif crops: Sown in monsoon (June-July) and harvested in autumn (Sept-Oct), e.g., rice, maize, jowar, bajra.
IMD: Principal agency responsible for meteorological observations, weather forecasting, and seismology in India.
Impact of unseasonal rain: Crop lodging, pest attacks, delayed harvesting, reduced yield.
mainsPoints: Impact of climate change on agriculture: Changing rainfall patterns, extreme weather events, effects on crop cycles and yields.
Agricultural resilience: Need for crop diversification, climate-resilient varieties, and improved irrigation techniques.
Role of weather forecasting: Importance of accurate and timely advisories for farmers and disaster management.
Government schemes: PM Fasal Bima Yojana, National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) to support farmers.
Food security implications: Ensuring stable agricultural production amidst climatic variability.
GS1.imageDescription: A wheat field in Northern India with light rain falling.
GS1.trivia: The term 'Western Disturbance' was coined by Indian meteorologists in the early 20th century.

History of India and Indian National Movement
